sections.content:
**A system prompt is context you set once that applies to every response in a session.**

Instead of re-explaining your role, company, and preferences every time, you can write a system prompt (also called a "custom instructions" or "pre-prompt") that establishes the baseline for all interactions.

Effective system prompts include:
- **Who you are:** Your role, industry, experience level
- **What you're working on:** Current project, company type, team size
- **Communication preferences:** Format defaults, level of detail, formality
- **What to avoid:** Things AI tends to do that annoy you (excessive caveats, overly formal tone, etc.)

Example system prompt for a product manager:
> I'm a Senior PM at a Series B SaaS company (100 employees, HR tech). When I ask for documents, default to bullet points unless I say otherwise. Skip lengthy preambles — get to the content quickly. When you're unsure, ask a clarifying question rather than guessing. Avoid corporate jargon.

Once set, every interaction in that session benefits from this context without you needing to re-state it.

sections.bad: Starting every conversation by typing 3 paragraphs of background before getting to your actual question.
sections.good: Setting a system prompt once: 'I'm a marketing director at a fintech startup. My audiences are: executives (need high-level), dev teams (need technical precision), customers (need plain English). When I ask for writing, always ask which audience first unless it's obvious from context.' Then asking: 'Draft an announcement for the new API rate limits.'
sections.explanation: Without a system prompt, every conversation requires re-establishing context. With a system prompt, AI already knows your role, audiences, and preferences. The AI in the example will now automatically ask 'which audience?' before writing the announcement — which is exactly the behavior you want, with no additional prompting.

sections.prompt: Write a system prompt for yourself that you could use at the start of a working session. Include: your role/context, your preferred output format, at least one thing AI should always ask before doing, and one habit to avoid.

rubric: A strong system prompt includes role and industry context, specific format preferences (default to bullets? short answers?), a clarifying question trigger, and at least one explicit avoidance instruction
It should be clear and usable — not a paragraph of aspirations
data.exampleAnswer: I'm a Head of Customer Success at a 80-person B2B SaaS company. Default format: bullet points with bold key terms. Length: concise unless I ask for depth. Before drafting any customer-facing communication, ask me: what's the customer's sentiment and desired outcome? Avoid: starting responses with 'Certainly!' or 'Of course!', and skip the 'I hope this helps' sign-offs.

sections.prompt: What's the main benefit of a system prompt compared to including context in each individual prompt?

choices: You only have to define your context once, and it applies to every response in the session — saving time and improving consistency
System prompts override AI's safety guidelines
System prompts are permanent across all future sessions
data.explanation: System prompts set context once for an entire session. Instead of re-explaining your role, preferences, and constraints in every prompt, you define them at the start and AI applies them consistently. Note: they're typically session-scoped (not permanent) — check your specific tool's implementation.
